content.text: The fundamental distinction between the joint distribution p(x, y) and the conditional distribution p(y | x) underlies nearly every design decision in machine learning. Choosing which distribution to model determines what a system can do, what data it needs, and how it will fail. Understanding how these two distributions relate — and when the difference matters — is essential for reasoning about generative AI, probabilistic models, and the limits of any learned system.
Definitions and the Fundamental Relationship
For random variables X (input) and Y (output or label), three distributions are in play:
Distribution
Notation
What it models
Joint
p(x, y)
Probability of x and y co-occurring
Conditional
p(y | x)
Probability of y given x is observed
Marginal
p(x) or p(y)
Probability of x (or y) alone, integrating out the other
They are linked by the product rule and Bayes' theorem:

attrs.latex: p(x, y) = p(y \mid x)\, p(x) = p(x \mid y)\, p(y)

content.text: A model of the joint p(x, y) implicitly contains both conditionals p(y | x) and p(x | y), and both marginals p(x) and p(y). A model of the conditional p(y | x) contains none of the others — it is the minimum sufficient quantity for classification, and nothing more.
What Each Distribution Lets You Do

import torch
import torch.distributions as D


# ── Example: 2D Gaussian joint distribution ──────────────────────────────────
#
# Suppose X = pixel brightness, Y = class label (cat=0, dog=1)
# Joint: p(x, y) = p(x | y) * p(y)
# Conditional: p(y | x) derived via Bayes

class JointGaussian:
    """
    Illustrative joint distribution p(x, y) for a 2-class problem.
    Generative model: models both p(x | y) and p(y).
    """
    def __init__(self):
        # Prior over classes (dogs are more common)
        self.p_y = D.Categorical(probs=torch.tensor([0.4, 0.6]))
        # Class-conditional distributions p(x | y)
        self.p_x_given_y = [
            D.Normal(loc=torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0]), scale=torch.tensor([0.5, 0.5])),  # cats
            D.Normal(loc=torch.tensor([3.0, 1.0]), scale=torch.tensor([0.5, 0.5])),  # dogs
        ]

    def sample(self, n: int):
        """Sample from joint: first draw y, then x | y."""
        y = self.p_y.sample((n,))    # (n,) class labels
        x = torch.stack([
            self.p_x_given_y[y_i.item()].sample() for y_i in y
        ])                            # (n, 2) inputs
        return x, y

    def log_joint(self, x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """log p(x, y) = log p(x | y) + log p(y)"""
        log_p_y = self.p_y.log_prob(y)   # (n,)
        log_p_x_given_y = torch.stack([
            self.p_x_given_y[y_i.item()].log_prob(x[i]).sum()
            for i, y_i in enumerate(y)
        ])                                # (n,)
        return log_p_x_given_y + log_p_y

    def log_conditional(self, x: torch.Tensor, y_query: int) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        log p(y=y_query | x) via Bayes' theorem.
        Requires both class-conditional likelihoods — not possible without the
        full joint model.
        """
        log_priors = self.p_y.logits - torch.logsumexp(self.p_y.logits, dim=0)
        log_likelihoods = torch.stack([
            dist.log_prob(x).sum() for dist in self.p_x_given_y
        ])  # (K,)
        log_joint_per_class = log_likelihoods + log_priors
        log_p_x = torch.logsumexp(log_joint_per_class, dim=0)
        return log_joint_per_class[y_query] - log_p_x  # Bayes denominator

    def sample_x_given_y(self, y: int, n: int = 1) -> torch.Tensor:
        """Sample new inputs for a specific class — generation."""
        return self.p_x_given_y[y].sample((n,))
Why the Joint Is Strictly More Informative
The joint p(x, y) contains strictly more information than p(y | x). From the joint you can derive:
p(y | x) by normalising over y — for classification.
p(x | y) by normalising over x — for conditional generation ("draw me a cat").
p(x) by marginalising over y — for density estimation and anomaly detection.
p(y) by marginalising over x — the marginal class distribution.
p(x_missing | x_observed, y) — imputation under the model.
From p(y | x) alone, none of the above are derivable. The conditional is the minimum sufficient statistic for the prediction task, and its compactness is exactly what makes it efficient to learn.
The Price of Modelling the Full Joint

Modelling p(x, y) correctly requires the model to represent the marginal p(x) — the full complexity of the input space. For high-dimensional inputs like images, text, or audio, this is an enormous challenge: the model must capture all modes of variation in x whether or not that variation is relevant to predicting y. Features like lighting direction in images, rare synonyms in text, or background noise in audio contribute to p(x) but may be irrelevant to the classification task.

This means that a generative classifier — which uses p(y | x) derived via Bayes from a joint model — can be beaten by a discriminative classifier that models p(y | x) directly, simply because the discriminative model wastes no capacity on irrelevant variation in x. This is the mathematical basis for Vapnik's principle: "do not solve a harder problem as an intermediate step to solving an easier one."
When Joint Modelling Wins
Despite this argument for discriminative models, there are situations where the joint model wins:
Scenario
Why joint modelling helps
Low labelled data
Unlabelled examples can train p(x); the marginal class prior p(y) informs classification
Class imbalance
p(y) is part of the model; rare-class priors are explicitly represented
Missing features at test time
p(y | x_observed) = Σ_{x_missing} p(y | x) · p(x_missing | x_observed) — requires joint
Detecting distribution shift
Low p(x) at test time signals OOD — not possible with p(y | x) alone
Counterfactual reasoning
"What would this x look like if y were different?" requires p(x | y)
The Special Case: Unsupervised Generative Models

When there are no labels (y is absent), the only distribution to model is the marginal p(x). Language models, image generative models, and audio synthesis models are all in this category. The objective is:

Here the joint/conditional distinction collapses: there is only the marginal p(x) to model. Conditioning can be introduced post-hoc — a conditional generation model p(x | c) where c is a text prompt, class label, or style specification is still fundamentally a generative model of p(x, c) marginalised appropriately. GPT-style models learn p(x_{1:T}) as a joint over token sequences, but any prefix can serve as a conditioning context.
Bayes-Optimal Classifiers: Both Representations Are Equivalent

With infinite data and a perfectly expressive model, the Bayes-optimal classifier from a joint model and from a conditional model are identical — both converge to the true p(y | x). The difference is purely in the estimation problem given finite data. A model of p(x, y) has more parameters to estimate (it must also fit p(x)), leading to higher variance but also capturing more structure. A model of p(y | x) has fewer free parameters and lower variance but cannot extrapolate to generative tasks.

import torch


def bayes_optimal_from_joint(
    log_p_x_given_y: torch.Tensor,   # (n, K) log p(x | y=k) for each sample
    log_p_y: torch.Tensor,           # (K,) log p(y=k)
) -> torch.Tensor:
    """
    Compute p(y | x) via Bayes' theorem from a joint generative model.
    Same result as a discriminative model in the large-data limit.

    log_p_x_given_y: (n, K) — one score per sample per class
    log_p_y:         (K,)   — log prior over classes
    returns:         (n, K) — posterior p(y | x)
    """
    # log p(x, y=k) = log p(x | y=k) + log p(y=k)
    log_joint = log_p_x_given_y + log_p_y.unsqueeze(0)   # (n, K)
    # log p(y=k | x) = log p(x, y=k) - log p(x)
    #                 = log_joint - logsumexp over k
    log_p_x = torch.logsumexp(log_joint, dim=1, keepdim=True)  # (n, 1)
    log_posterior = log_joint - log_p_x                        # (n, K)
    return log_posterior.exp()                                 # (n, K)


# Both approaches predict the same class under identical generative assumptions:
# argmax_y p(y | x)  ==  argmax_y [log p(x | y) + log p(y)]  (Bayes)
# argmax_y f_theta(x)[y]                                       (discriminative)
#
# They differ only in how much structure is assumed / estimated from data.

Mental model: the joint p(x, y) is the complete blueprint of the data — it specifies everything about how inputs and labels co-occur. The conditional p(y | x) is derived from this blueprint by fixing x and asking which labels are plausible. Modelling the joint is like building a complete city map; modelling the conditional is like memorising only the fastest route from A to B. The route is all you need for navigation, but the full map enables many more queries — the trade-off is how much effort it takes to build.
Key Takeaways

The joint p(x, y) = p(y | x) · p(x) = p(x | y) · p(y) subsumes all three other distributions; the conditional p(y | x) is the minimum needed for classification and cannot be inverted to recover p(x, y).
Discriminative models estimate p(y | x) directly — efficient but cannot generate x, detect OOD inputs, or handle missing features without extra machinery.
Generative models estimate p(x, y) or p(x) — richer but harder to estimate; the benefit appears in low-data regimes, semi-supervised settings, and generative tasks.
With infinite data, both approaches converge to the same Bayes-optimal classifier; the difference is finite-sample estimation efficiency.

Modern large language models train on the unsupervised objective log p(x) over unlabelled text, learning the full joint over token sequences — making them generative models that can be steered into discriminative behaviour at inference time.
